title: Key Decisions

jokes.text: Great. So now we don't have a car or a jet? Why don't we just take an ad out in I'm Poor magazine?
jokes.setup: Michael explains they need to cut back, including selling the corporate jet
jokes.punchline: Lindsay imagines 'I'm Poor magazine' as if poverty is a niche lifestyle brand — delivered with total obliviousness to her own privilege
jokes.explanation: A sharp, character-specific joke. 'I'm Poor magazine' is a genuinely original comic invention — it perfectly encapsulates Lindsay's wealthy obliviousness by treating poverty as a consumer identity. Only Lindsay could say this. The economy is excellent; the entire joke is one sentence. Comparable in quality to the 8.0 anchor.

jokes.text: What about the trees? — We're just gonna cover them up with blankets.
jokes.setup: Lindsay objects to bulldozing trees for development
jokes.punchline: Michael's absurd, deadpan non-solution — covering trees with blankets — delivered as if it's a reasonable plan
jokes.explanation: A quick absurdist beat. The blanket solution is silly and lands on the surprise of Michael briefly playing dumb before clarifying he'll rip them out. Works as a fast gag but doesn't deeply integrate character beyond 'exasperated brother.'

jokes.text: I'll never forget your wedding. [in response to Lindsay mentioning Neuterfest]
jokes.setup: Lindsay claims she's always been deeply passionate about nature and invokes 'Neuterfest' as evidence
jokes.punchline: Michael's deadpan response conflates Neuterfest with a wedding, implying it was a significant enough event to remember — and the name 'Neuterfest' itself is the joke
jokes.explanation: A masterclass in economy. 'Neuterfest' is a comic invention that implies an entire absurd world — a neutering festival significant enough to be confused with a wedding. Michael's flat response ('I'll never forget your wedding') does triple duty: mocks Lindsay's activism, suggests Neuterfest was chaotic and ceremony-like, and lands in one short sentence. Exceptional craft. The name alone earns high originality.

jokes.text: I care deeply for nature. — You're wearing ostrich skin boots. — I don't care about ostriches.
jokes.setup: Lindsay insists she cares deeply for nature while Michael notes the contradiction
jokes.punchline: Lindsay's complete non-apology: she simply carves out ostriches as an exception with zero self-awareness
jokes.explanation: The punchline is the specificity of the exception. Lindsay doesn't say 'they're not really nature' or get defensive — she just flatly excludes ostriches from her compassion. The three-line structure is airtight: setup, contradiction, dismissal. Strong character integration; this is pure Lindsay Bluth logic.

jokes.text: It's bad enough that I gotta go to the awards show tomorrow night. Today I gotta stand next to her like I'm Rita Wilson.
jokes.setup: Gob complains about being overshadowed at Marta's TV appearance
jokes.punchline: Comparing himself to Rita Wilson (Tom Hanks' wife, known for standing next to a more famous spouse) — Gob's ego is so massive that being supportive feels like a personal affront
jokes.explanation: The Rita Wilson reference is smart because it's specific enough to be funny without being a cheap name-drop — Gob is genuinely horrified at the idea of being anyone's supporting player. Strong character integration. The reference works because Rita Wilson's public identity IS being 'Tom Hanks' wife,' which mirrors Gob's fear exactly.

jokes.text: Michael! — That's Mom. Hide.
jokes.setup: Michael is giving Gob sincere relationship advice about commitment and family
jokes.punchline: The moment Lucille calls, Michael immediately pivots to 'Hide' — abandoning all sincerity, revealing that even HE is afraid of their mother
jokes.explanation: The comedy is entirely in the whiplash. Michael's been playing the responsible adult for a full scene and the instant Lucille appears, he becomes a child again. 'Hide' is two syllables and it recontextualizes everything we just heard. Excellent economy.

jokes.text: I may not have her money anymore, but at least I have a live husband.
jokes.setup: Lucille is explaining why she wants to attend the Desi awards to hold her head high vs. Lucille Austero
jokes.punchline: Lucille's definition of 'winning' against her friend is that her husband is in prison rather than dead — a remarkably low bar delivered with complete confidence
jokes.explanation: The joke hinges on Lucille's twisted value system — 'live husband' is the only category in which she can claim superiority, and she announces it with triumph. The dark subversion is that prison is the baseline for a 'good' marriage in this family. Rewatch bonus because the callback to this payoff at the Desi awards pays it off further.

jokes.text: Make it long so Buster doesn't seem so... round.
jokes.setup: Lucille asks Michael to tie Buster's tie before the awards show
jokes.punchline: Lucille's offhand, clinical description of her adult son's body as 'round,' delivered as a styling note rather than an insult
jokes.explanation: Pure Lucille cruelty delivered as practical advice. The pause before 'round' is key — she's searching for the word that is simultaneously the most accurate and most cutting. Strong character integration; this is exactly how Lucille thinks about her children.

jokes.text: It's his glasses. They make him look like a lizard.
jokes.setup: Michael asks when Buster last went on a date, suggesting Buster should be with women his own age. Lucille has just said 'He's a beautiful boy. They don't appreciate him.'
jokes.punchline: Lucille undermines her own defense of Buster by casually noting his glasses make him look like a lizard — she simultaneously defends and insults him
jokes.explanation: The double-move makes this exceptional: Lucille defends Buster ('beautiful boy') then immediately supplies the reason he can't get dates ('makes him look like a lizard'). She doesn't see the contradiction. 'Lizard' is a perfectly specific, unexpected animal choice. High character integration — this is Lucille's conditional love made literal.

jokes.text: Gee, I wonder why. [after Lucille says Buster is self-conscious]
jokes.setup: Lucille has just said Buster is self-conscious about his glasses after calling him round and lizard-like
jokes.punchline: Michael's dry response — the obvious observation that Lucille herself is the source of Buster's self-consciousness
jokes.explanation: A functional straight-man line. The sarcasm is clear and the timing is good, but it's the kind of line any sensible observer could deliver. Works as a punctuation beat on the stronger jokes around it.

jokes.text: If that's a veiled criticism about me, I won't hear it and I won't respond to it.
jokes.setup: Michael just asked when Buster last went on a date, implying Buster should spend time with non-maternal women
jokes.punchline: Lucille's response is itself a response to the criticism she claims she won't respond to, making the logic self-defeating — and she has zero awareness of this
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best lines. The paradox is built into the sentence: she IS responding to the criticism she says she won't respond to. The self-defeating logic is both linguistically clever and a perfect encapsulation of Lucille's psychology — she cannot admit vulnerability even while demonstrating it. Only Lucille Bluth could deliver this line. Approaches the 9.5 calibration anchor in craft terms.

jokes.text: Watch out for bridges and hop-ons. You're gonna get some hop-ons.
jokes.setup: Michael has traded the jet for a stair car — a wheeled vehicle with institutional stairs attached — and is now stuck using it as the family vehicle
jokes.punchline: Lucille's completely matter-of-fact warning about 'hop-ons' — strangers who will opportunistically board a moving stair car — delivered as if this is just a known fact of stair car ownership
jokes.explanation: The word 'hop-ons' is a genuine comic invention — a new noun for a new social category. The joke works on multiple levels: the absurdity of the stair car itself, the specificity of the hazard, and the casual certainty with which Lucille delivers it ('you're gonna get some'). This is also a running gag that pays off in later episodes; rewatch bonus is high. Among the most quotable lines in the episode.

jokes.text: After we get that lot cleared, we're gonna have enough money for you to neuter thousands of animals. You can make dogs and cats a complete thing of the past. No more dogs and cats.
jokes.setup: Michael is trying to get Lindsay to help remove the activist from the tree by appealing to her cause
jokes.punchline: Michael's escalating framing of Lindsay's cause — from neutering to species elimination — 'No more dogs and cats' as if that's the end goal of animal welfare
jokes.explanation: The escalation from 'neuter thousands' to 'no more dogs and cats' as a selling point is darkly funny — Michael is accidentally describing an extinction event as a benefit. Callbacks to Neuterfest. The rapid logical leap is the joke.

jokes.text: I'll do it for the cats.
jokes.setup: Michael has framed getting rid of trees as enabling Lindsay to 'make dogs and cats a complete thing of the past'
jokes.punchline: Lindsay agrees — for the cats specifically, implying she wants dogs eliminated but has some preference for cats — a bizarre non-sequitur that reveals she has entirely missed the point
jokes.explanation: Four words that do enormous work. Lindsay accepts Michael's absurd framing completely and adds a specific preference ('for the cats') that implies she's fine with canine extinction. Perfect economy. Deeply character-specific — Lindsay's causes are always about what she feels in the moment, not any coherent ideology.

jokes.text: I, too, have an announcement to make. To prove that no prison can hold me, I will incarcerate myself in the penitentiary that holds my own father, only to escape 24 hours later.
jokes.setup: Marta is giving a heartfelt interview about family being more important than career; the camera is on her
jokes.punchline: Gob interrupts with a completely unrelated magic stunt announcement — the content of the stunt is absurd (going to prison voluntarily to prove prison can't hold him), and the timing shows he has not been listening at all
jokes.explanation: The joke works on three levels: (1) Gob hijacking Marta's emotional moment with showbiz; (2) the logic of his stunt — going TO prison to prove prison can't hold you; (3) 'the penitentiary that holds my own father' delivered as dramatic flair without any apparent awareness that this is strange. Strong character integration. Rewatch bonus because the stunt drives the entire episode plot.

jokes.text: Say that to them in Spanish.
jokes.setup: Gob has just interrupted Marta's interview with his prison escape announcement and the declaration 'No shackles can hold these hands'
jokes.punchline: The interviewer's straight-faced instruction to translate it — treating Gob's self-aggrandizement as interview content worth continuing
jokes.explanation: A quick deadpan beat. The interviewer's complete acceptance of Gob's nonsense is funny but the laugh is mild — it's more of a grace note than a joke in its own right.

jokes.text: What? No, Dad's fine. Just saw him in there throwing the ball around with a couple guys. Which, of course, he never did with me.
jokes.setup: Michael rushes to the prison assuming something is wrong with their father
jokes.punchline: Gob casually reports seeing their incarcerated father 'throwing the ball around' — a normal, wholesome activity — and then immediately notes with suppressed bitterness that George Sr. never did this with him
jokes.explanation: The laugh is the pivot — the completely matter-of-fact reporting of prison life as 'throwing the ball around' followed by the sudden vulnerability of 'which he never did with me.' The casual delivery of childhood trauma is a recurring Arrested Development signature. Rewatch bonus because this plants the catch storyline paid off later.

jokes.text: The warden was intrigued... less about the stunt... and more about the prison beatings this brash magician was sure to receive.
jokes.setup: The warden is agreeing to let Gob attempt a prison escape stunt, saying 'any press is good press'
jokes.punchline: The narrator reveals the warden's ACTUAL motivation — anticipating Gob getting beaten up — delivered in the same flat documentary tone
jokes.explanation: The narrator's aside is classically Arrested Development — the flat documentary voice undercutting the apparent meaning of a scene. The gap between what the warden says and what the narrator reveals is the joke. 'Prison beatings this brash magician was sure to receive' is both dark and perfectly calibrated.

jokes.text: Well, any press is good press, huh? / Right. [beat]
jokes.setup: The narrator has just revealed the warden wants Gob to get beaten. Gob cheerfully says 'any press is good press.'
jokes.punchline: The warden's 'Right' — knowing he wants Gob beaten — followed by an implied uncomfortable beat, is the joke. He's agreeing for entirely different reasons than Gob thinks.
jokes.explanation: A layered irony joke — the warden's agreement is loaded with meaning Gob can't access. Works better on rewatch when you know the warden's true motivation. Decent economy.

jokes.text: So Gob went about swallowing the key to prepare for his escape. [followed by ~30 seconds of implied key-swallowing action before Gob speaks again]
jokes.setup: Gob explains his escape plan involves swallowing the cell key and then passing it later
jokes.punchline: The act of swallowing the key — presumably a large, metal key — shown/implied with apparent difficulty, establishing the farcical logistics of the plan
jokes.explanation: A physical comedy setup that pays off elaborately later. The visual implied by the transcript — a man laboriously swallowing a large prison key in front of a warden and Michael — is inherently absurd and the timestamp gap suggests a prolonged, uncomfortable visual sequence.

jokes.text: Of course, that is gonna conflict with my going to the Desis with Marta. — Well, life's a compromise.
jokes.setup: Gob has just swallowed a key and committed to a prison escape stunt that will take 24 hours
jokes.punchline: He frames missing his girlfriend's awards ceremony — because he put himself in prison — as a normal life compromise, and Michael's 'life's a compromise' is a straight-faced acceptance of this logic
jokes.explanation: The comedy is in Gob's breezy acceptance that his self-inflicted prison sentence creates a scheduling conflict. 'Life's a compromise' from Michael is a tired phrase that takes on absurdist weight in context. Solid but not exceptional.

jokes.text: I probably would have taken her to the nice event, but prison'll be fun too.
jokes.setup: Gob has chosen his magic prison escape stunt over attending Marta's awards ceremony with her
jokes.punchline: Gob rationalizes 'prison'll be fun too' as if both options are roughly equivalent evening activities
jokes.explanation: The joke is Gob's complete moral inversion — prison is an acceptable substitute for a romantic evening — delivered with total self-assurance. Good character work but follows closely on similar Gob beats, so it doesn't land as fresh.

jokes.text: Thank you. I knew you'd come through. / How'd I come through? / Gob, how'd I come through?
jokes.setup: Gob thanks Michael for 'coming through' for him. Michael has no idea what Gob means.
jokes.punchline: Michael's confused repetition of 'how'd I come through?' while Gob gives no answer reveals that Gob has unilaterally decided Michael is handling something (taking Marta to the awards) without telling him.
jokes.explanation: The repetition of 'how'd I come through?' with Gob not answering is a neat structural joke — the audience understands what happened (Gob volunteered Michael without asking) while Michael is left in the dark. Good use of withheld information as comedy.

jokes.text: You might wanna find something a little stronger, Warden. [referring to the warden's drink]
jokes.setup: Gob has just blundered — he's committed to the prison plan without thinking through that he's also committed to take Marta to the Desis (by volunteering Michael as her escort)
jokes.punchline: Michael's understated advice to the warden implies the situation is more disastrous than it appears; also works as Michael processing his own new obligation
jokes.explanation: A functional 'this is going to be bad' punctuation joke. Works in context but doesn't generate its own comedic energy — it's a straight-man reaction beat.

jokes.text: Get out of our tree.
jokes.setup: Lindsay has been sent by Michael to remove an environmental activist from a tree that needs to be cleared for their development project
jokes.punchline: Lindsay's approach: she simply tells Johnny Bark to get out 'of our tree' — asserting property ownership while ostensibly representing environmentalism, with zero self-awareness about the contradiction
jokes.explanation: Four words that perfectly embody Lindsay's character: she frames the activist as a trespasser rather than engaging with his cause at all. 'Our tree' signals ownership while the mission is to destroy it. Peak Lindsay contradiction delivered with maximum economy.

jokes.text: Keep your arm up. That's how you get accuracy. — Thought you said throwing the ball against the garage door by yourself was how you got accuracy.
jokes.setup: George Sr. is coaching inmates on throwing a ball; Gob shows up and challenges his advice
jokes.punchline: Gob reveals George Sr. told him (as a child) to practice alone — i.e., George Sr. told his son a false tip to avoid playing with him
jokes.explanation: A dark joke with perfect structural setup. George Sr.'s coaching of random inmates is immediately undercut by the revelation that he told his own son to practice alone — against a garage door — to get him out of the way. The specificity ('garage door by yourself') makes it land hard. Strong character integration on both sides: George Sr.'s neglect and Gob's wounded obliviousness.

jokes.text: I killed a guy. Nah, it's just a publicity stunt.
jokes.setup: Gob answers his father's question 'what are you doing here?' — George Sr. is in prison, so the logical answer from a cellmate would be crime
jokes.punchline: Gob plays into the expectation ('I killed a guy') then immediately deflates it — but the misdirection itself reveals how performative and theatrical Gob's entire existence is
jokes.explanation: The misdirection is fast and clean. 'I killed a guy' lands as a punchline before being revealed as a setup for the actual punchline. Also works because Gob's instinct is always to make an entrance — even with his imprisoned father.

jokes.text: Oh, I'm sorry. I didn't realize there weren't any magic fans here.
jokes.setup: George Sr. tells Gob 'You idiot, you're gonna get yourself killed'
jokes.punchline: Gob interprets his father's fear for his safety as a cultural failing — the problem is the audience, not the plan
jokes.explanation: The joke is Gob's complete inability to receive criticism — he reframes paternal concern as a problem of the venue's demographic. 'Magic fans' as a category of people who would accept his plan is perfectly character-integrated. Only Gob Bluth operates this way.

jokes.text: Hey, White Power Bill, he's all right. — White Power Bill can't hear you... with such dirty ears. — Hey, White Power Bill has dirty ears. Hey, guys! Dirty Ears Bill! Dirty Ears Bill!
jokes.setup: Gob is trying to fit in with the prison population; George Sr. introduces him to a white supremacist named 'White Power Bill'
jokes.punchline: Gob immediately rebrands the white supremacist as 'Dirty Ears Bill' based on a hygiene observation — completely oblivious to the danger — and begins rallying the prison population around the new nickname
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's funniest sustained beat. The escalation from introduction → observation → renaming → crowd-rallying is perfectly structured. The joke has multiple layers: (1) the dark premise of a character named 'White Power Bill' being treated as normal; (2) Gob's total obliviousness to danger; (3) the specific hygiene observation as a basis for the rename; (4) Gob's instinct to perform and get a crowd going — even in prison with a white supremacist. 'Dirty Ears Bill!' chanted in a prison yard is both deeply absurd and perfectly escalated. Approaches the 9.5 calibration anchor.

jokes.text: You're Marta? [Michael's visible shock at how attractive Marta is]
jokes.setup: Gob has been complaining about his girlfriend and treating their relationship as a burden; Michael has agreed to escort Marta to the awards show as a favor
jokes.punchline: Michael's stunned 'You're Marta?' reveals that Gob's girlfriend is far more impressive than Gob's complaints suggested — and plants the episode's central romantic complication
jokes.explanation: The reaction beat does heavy lifting for the episode's plot. The comedy is partly in the gap between how Gob described Marta and what Michael discovers; rewatch bonus because this moment seeds the Michael/Marta arc that runs through multiple episodes.

jokes.text: Listen, Marta, I hope you don't mind driving. I took a cab here. — Well, my brother may have eaten them. [Michael explaining he couldn't find his keys]
jokes.setup: Michael awkwardly explains he has no keys and therefore no way to drive
jokes.punchline: Michael's explanation — delivered completely seriously — that his brother may have literally eaten his keys
jokes.explanation: A clean callback to Gob swallowing the key. The comedy comes from Michael delivering 'my brother may have eaten them' as a plausible explanation — not embarrassed, just factual. The calm acceptance of his brother's insanity is a great Michael Bluth character beat.

jokes.text: I haven't hired a limo or anything. — I don't mind. — I think you might. [followed by the stair car pulling up]
jokes.setup: Michael warns Marta that their transportation isn't great
jokes.punchline: The reveal of the stair car — an enormous vehicle with a retractable airport staircase — as the date vehicle for a glamorous awards show
jokes.explanation: The stair car visual gag pays off the setup from earlier. The structure is excellent — Michael's understatement ('I think you might') followed by the reveal. The hop-on warning earlier makes this funnier in retrospect. Rewatch bonus because the stair car becomes a recurring prop across the series.

jokes.text: Look, I'm an activist too. I appreciate what you're doing for the environment. But we're not the only ones who destroy trees. What about beavers? You call yourself an environmentalist? Go club some beavers.
jokes.setup: Lindsay is attempting to reason with Johnny Bark the tree activist
jokes.punchline: Lindsay's entirely incoherent environmental argument pivots from 'beavers also destroy trees' to 'go club some beavers' — advocating wildlife clubbing as environmental activism
jokes.explanation: Lindsay's argument is simultaneously her most passionate and most self-defeating. The logic has an internal structure (beavers = tree destroyers = club them) that parodies activist reasoning while being completely insane. 'Go club some beavers' as an environmental rallying cry is both absurd and character-perfect — Lindsay has always been an activist in aesthetic only. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: Where are the stairs? [Michael arriving at the Desi awards, realizing there are no exterior stairs — because he has the stair car]
jokes.setup: Michael has driven the stair car to the awards show; now that they've arrived, he needs to get down from the vehicle
jokes.punchline: Michael asking 'where are the stairs?' while presumably standing at the top of the stair car — the stairs are what he's ON, or the meta-absurdity of needing stairs to exit the stair car
jokes.explanation: The stair car gag continues. The punchline is the logical confusion — the stair car IS stairs — or the absurdity of arriving at a glamorous event on an airport vehicle. Rewatch bonus as part of the stair car running gag.

jokes.text: A sea of waiters, and no one will take a drink order.
jokes.setup: Lucille is at the Desi awards, surrounded by what she assumes are waiters
jokes.punchline: The implied joke is that she's at a Latino television event and the people she's trying to flag down for drinks are not waiters — they are guests, attendees, or celebrities. Lucille's racism is exposed through her inability to imagine anyone around her isn't in service.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's sharpest jokes. The line never explicitly states what Lucille is doing wrong — it trusts the audience to make the connection. The horror is in the plausible deniability: she just wants a drink, but the framing indicts her completely. Perfectly economical — one sentence does all the work. Only Lucille Bluth would say this and mean it sincerely. Approaches the 8.0+ threshold convincingly.

jokes.text: Buster mingling at the Desi awards without his glasses, singing/humming '¿Cómo estoy?' to himself
jokes.setup: Lucille insisted Buster not wear his glasses; Buster is severely visually impaired without them
jokes.punchline: Buster's confident, cheerful presence at an event he literally cannot see — '¿Cómo estoy?' ('How am I doing?') asked to no one — emphasizes both his helplessness and his oblivious cheerfulness
jokes.explanation: The visual comedy relies on Buster's blindness being played straight. '¿Cómo estoy?' is funny because it's the wrong question (it should be '¿Cómo estás?') AND he's asking it to himself while unable to see anything around him. Small but effective character beat.

jokes.text: Showing up here without your husband. Shame be damned. Caution to the wind. — That's so sweet, darling. I'm here to support you. — You're the one who's all alone and likely to stay that way. — My husband's just a phone call away. — That's one call per day, isn't it? Gee, I should think he'd wanna save that for his lawyer. — At least he's in prison, not an urn.
jokes.setup: The two Lucilles meet at the Desi awards — ostensibly friends, but clearly rivals
jokes.punchline: The entire exchange is a rapid-fire passive-aggressive escalation: Austero mocks Lucille for coming without her husband; Lucille pivots to 'he's just a phone call away'; Austero points out the one-call-per-day prison restriction; Lucille counters with 'at least he's in prison, not an urn' — winning the exchange by celebrating her husband's imprisonment over Austero's widowhood
jokes.explanation: This is a masterfully constructed escalation sequence. Each line one-ups the previous in cruelty and specificity. The callback to Lucille's earlier boast about her 'live husband' makes the final punchline ('at least he's in prison, not an urn') even richer — she's been preparing for this moment. 'In prison, not an urn' as a flex is genuinely brilliant dark comedy. Both characters are revealed in their awfulness simultaneously.

jokes.text: You are so deliciously witty. Aren't we having fun?
jokes.setup: Lucille Bluth has just told Lucille Austero 'at least he's in prison, not an urn' — a viciously cutting remark
jokes.punchline: Lucille Austero responds with warmth and genuine delight — she LOVES being brutalized by Lucille Bluth, which makes their relationship even more disturbing
jokes.explanation: The joke is that Lucille Austero is delighted rather than wounded. This reframes the entire exchange — these two have been doing this for decades and Austero relishes it. 'Aren't we having fun?' is both sincere and devastating. Adds character depth to what could have been a one-sided attack.

jokes.text: The other Lucille also suffers from severe vertigo. [followed by Lucille Austero losing her balance at the event]
jokes.setup: The narrator introduces Lucille Austero's vertigo as a character trait mid-scene
jokes.punchline: The reveal is timed so that Lucille Austero immediately demonstrates the vertigo — a classic Arrested Development technique of mentioning a condition and instantly showing its consequences
jokes.explanation: The narrator's aside followed by immediate physical consequence is a signature AD structural joke. The timing — introduce condition, then immediately demonstrate it — is tight and funny. Rewatch bonus because the vertigo becomes a recurring gag for Lucille Austero across the series.

jokes.text: Lucille Austero first took notice of Buster [visual: she sees him without his glasses for the first time and finds him attractive] — Buster, however, failed to notice her [visual: Buster walking past, oblivious due to blindness without glasses]
jokes.setup: Buster is at the event without glasses; Lucille Austero — who has vertigo — is also there
jokes.punchline: The romantic notice is completely one-sided for the most literal possible reason: Buster cannot see her without his glasses. The narrator states 'Buster, however, failed to notice her' as a pure physical fact.
jokes.explanation: The setup-payoff across the scene is elegant: Lucille made Buster remove his glasses, which means he literally cannot see the romantic opportunity created by those same missing glasses. The irony is structural and layered. Rewatch bonus because the Buster/Lucille Austero relationship becomes a major arc.

jokes.text: She sent him a proposition. [Lucille Austero sends a note to Buster via waiter]
jokes.setup: Lucille Austero is attracted to Buster and sends a note via waiter as a 'proposition'
jokes.punchline: Buster receives the note but appears confused — the 'proposition' framing sets up something risqué, but Buster's obliviousness and the context (an older woman hitting on a deeply mother-dependent man-child) is the comedy
jokes.explanation: Setup for a longer payoff. Works as a scene beat but the comedy here is mostly anticipatory — we know Buster's relationship with his mother makes this dynamic absurd. The rewatch bonus is high because the Buster/Lucille Austero relationship pays off richly later.

jokes.text: Did you win? I-I don't speak Spanish.
jokes.setup: Marta has just lost the Desi award; the ceremony was conducted in Spanish; Michael doesn't speak Spanish
jokes.punchline: Michael asks 'did you win?' — revealing he sat through the entire ceremony without understanding a word — delivered with complete unembarrassed honesty
jokes.explanation: The economy is excellent — one line reveals both his cluelessness and his decency (he came despite not understanding anything). The addition of 'I don't speak Spanish' as a plain explanation rather than an apology is a nice Michael Bluth character moment.

jokes.text: I can't pass this key without privacy!
jokes.setup: Gob is in prison and needs to retrieve the key he swallowed; the guards have locked the doors and he has no private bathroom access
jokes.punchline: Gob's anguished declaration — stated with the dignity of a man with principles — that he cannot physically pass the key in non-private conditions
jokes.explanation: The line works because Gob delivers it with such wounded dignity. The escalation from 'I need a private bathroom' to 'I can't pass this key without privacy!' recontextualizes the physical comedy in terms of Gob's character — even while trapped in his own absurd plan, he has standards. Highly quotable because of the specificity and the earnestness.

jokes.text: I've made a huge mistake.
jokes.setup: Gob is stuck in prison, unable to retrieve the key he swallowed because there's no private bathroom
jokes.punchline: Gob's understated self-assessment — 'I've made a huge mistake' — delivered at the moment of maximum physical discomfort and logistical failure
jokes.explanation: One of Arrested Development's most iconic running lines. The first utterance here establishes the phrase that becomes a series-wide recurring gag. The power is in the gap between the enormity of Gob's situation (alone in a prison, constipated, surrounded by inmates who want to beat him) and the blandness of 'huge mistake.' Economy is perfect. This line alone became a cultural touchstone — rewatch bonus is essential.

jokes.text: He was a serious and committed advocate for change. Where is he now? At a weekend stage fighting workshop with Carl Weathers.
jokes.setup: Lindsay is confiding in Johnny Bark that her husband Tobias used to be a committed activist like him, romanticizing her earlier relationship
jokes.punchline: The punchline deflates the romantic narrative — Tobias is now at 'a weekend stage fighting workshop with Carl Weathers,' which is both specific enough to be absurd and entirely Tobias-appropriate
jokes.explanation: The specificity of 'Carl Weathers' doing stage fighting workshops is the joke. Carl Weathers is real, and this is clearly a running gag that pays off in future episodes (Carl Weathers appears as a recurring character). The image of Tobias at a stage fighting workshop instead of meaningful activism perfectly encapsulates his character without him being present. Rewatch bonus because Carl Weathers becomes a recurring AD character.

jokes.text: Oh, I'm up here, Mike, and I'm not coming down! I'm gonna save this tree, no matter what it takes.
jokes.setup: Lindsay has been talking to Johnny Bark in the tree about lost idealism; Michael arrives and observes Lindsay in the tree
jokes.punchline: Lindsay suddenly performs passionate activism for Michael's benefit — a completely different person from the woman who just said she doesn't care anymore
jokes.explanation: The irony is that we just watched Lindsay confess her activist emptiness to Johnny Bark — and the moment Michael appears she performs the opposite. Good character integration but the joke is essentially 'Lindsay is a hypocrite,' which has already been established multiple times.

jokes.text: Okay. I'll see you when you realize what that bucket's for.
jokes.setup: Lindsay has declared she'll stay in the tree 'no matter what it takes'
jokes.punchline: Michael's calm, knowing reference to the bucket — implying the bathroom logistics of tree-sitting that Lindsay hasn't thought through
jokes.explanation: The economy is excellent — one line brings the entire activist fantasy crashing down into a toilet logistics problem. Michael doesn't explain it; he just references 'the bucket' with the confidence of someone who knows exactly how this ends. Perfectly understated.

jokes.text: I can see why your mom likes it. It is a really nice tree. We've gotta get it torn down. That must die... stupid tree.
jokes.setup: Michael is trying to get the tree cleared for development while also recognizing its actual appeal
jokes.punchline: Michael's escalating self-contradiction — from genuine appreciation ('really nice tree') to immediate reversal ('that must die') delivered with increasing desperation
jokes.explanation: The self-contradiction is funny — Michael is fighting his own instincts. 'Stupid tree' as an insult to an inanimate object at the end is a nice absurdist touch. Solid but not exceptional.

jokes.text: A waiter hands him a note; suddenly he's Steve McQueen.
jokes.setup: Lucille is complaining about Buster's reaction to receiving a note (proposition) from Lucille Austero at the Desi awards
jokes.punchline: Lucille compares Buster receiving a romantic note to Steve McQueen — the ultimate image of cool, masculine confidence — a ludicrous comparison to the helpless, Lucille-dependent Buster
jokes.explanation: The Steve McQueen comparison is the punchline — the gap between Steve McQueen's archetype (cool, rebellious, independent) and Buster's actual character (round, lizard-like, mother-dependent) is enormous. Lucille's jealousy driving her to mock her son's first romantic attention is peak character comedy.

jokes.text: He doesn't even know what she looks like. — I know she's a brownish area with points.
jokes.setup: Lucille points out that Buster can't even see Lucille Austero (because he's not wearing his glasses)
jokes.punchline: Buster's description of his romantic interest as 'a brownish area with points' — his literal visual impression of a human being filtered through extreme myopia
jokes.explanation: The episode's single best line. 'A brownish area with points' is a description of a human being so specific in its vagueness that it becomes transcendent. It perfectly encapsulates: (1) Buster's literal visual impairment without glasses; (2) the absurdity of falling for someone you can barely perceive; (3) the contrast between the romantic emotion Buster feels and the utter blankness of what he's actually seeing. Only Buster Bluth could say this. The economy is exceptional — five words replace an entire monologue. Approaches the 9.5 calibration anchor.

jokes.text: And I know I love her.
jokes.setup: Buster has described his romantic interest as 'a brownish area with points'
jokes.punchline: Buster immediately and earnestly declares love for this barely-perceived blur — the emotional sincerity is the joke
jokes.explanation: The immediate escalation from 'brownish area with points' to 'I know I love her' is the kicker that makes the sequence complete. Buster's earnest emotion is totally disproportionate to the information he has. Works as a two-punch combination with the previous line.

jokes.text: Okay, I don't know I love her... but I cannot tell you how liberating it is to be with someone who's not Mom... who's nothing like her.
jokes.setup: Buster is describing his attraction to Lucille Austero
jokes.punchline: Buster says Lucille Austero is 'nothing like' his mother — but Lucille Austero is an older woman named Lucille who is his mother's best friend, making her essentially a clone of his mother
jokes.explanation: The dramatic irony is rich: the audience knows Lucille Austero is basically Lucille Bluth's twin (same name, same social circle, same age, same aristocratic manner), and Buster's belief that she's 'nothing like' his mother is a stunning display of self-deception. The joke works on rewatch because as we learn more about Lucille Austero, Buster's blindness (literal and figurative) becomes even funnier.

jokes.text: That's what you do when life hands you a chance to be with someone special. You just grab that... that brownish area by its points, and you don't let go, no matter what your mom says.
jokes.setup: Buster is building to a motivational declaration about seizing romantic opportunity
jokes.punchline: Buster incorporates his visual description of Lucille Austero ('brownish area... points') into what should be a passionate romantic speech — it becomes an absurdist manifesto
jokes.explanation: The callback elevates this from a good line to an exceptional one. Buster takes the absurd visual description and uses it as the climax of his romantic declaration — 'grab that brownish area by its points and don't let go' is both genuinely moving (Buster's sincerity) and completely insane (the description). The addition of 'no matter what your mom says' undercuts the bravado with the reminder that Mom is still the real variable. One of the episode's strongest moments.

jokes.text: Good talk. [Michael, after Buster's passionate brownish area speech]
jokes.setup: Buster has just delivered an impassioned speech about grabbing the 'brownish area by its points'
jokes.punchline: Michael's completely flat 'Good talk' — the straight-man response that acknowledges nothing of what just happened
jokes.explanation: The perfect punctuation on the Buster sequence. Two words that say everything. Michael's 'Good talk' is ironic — it was not a good talk by any conventional measure — but his refusal to engage with Buster's reality is consistent characterization. Works best as the end of a sequence.

jokes.text: I unplugged the ice cream sandwich machine and the guards are all eating them before they get too melty, so I've got like a half an hour.
jokes.setup: Gob calls Michael from prison with a new escape plan update
jokes.punchline: Gob's improvised escape plan relies on guards being distracted by melting ice cream sandwiches — a specific, plausible-sounding, and completely undignified prison break strategy
jokes.explanation: The specificity is what makes it work. 'Before they get too melty' is a perfect comic detail — Gob's escape from a maximum security prison depends on a window determined by ice cream sandwich thermodynamics. Only Gob would devise this. Strong character integration.

jokes.text: Where the hell are the stairs? — Oh! I thought you were kidding!
jokes.setup: Gob has escaped to the prison wall and calls for Michael to bring the stair car to help him get over
jokes.punchline: Michael didn't bring the stairs because he thought Gob was joking — despite the elaborate escape plan that was clearly serious
jokes.explanation: The callback brings the stair car full circle into the escape plot. Michael's 'I thought you were kidding' is a reasonable response to an unreasonable situation, and highlights his complicity — he had the exact vehicle needed and just... didn't bring it. Works as a plot-comedy fusion.

jokes.text: Hey, speaking of kidding, how serious are you about Marta?
jokes.setup: Gob is desperately trying to escape prison by jumping off the wall; Michael has just said he thought Gob was kidding about needing the stairs
jokes.punchline: Michael uses 'speaking of kidding' as a pivot to ask whether Gob is serious about Marta — choosing THIS crisis moment to make his romantic inquiry, revealing how distracted he is by his feelings
jokes.explanation: The timing is the joke — Michael chooses the absolute worst moment to broach the Marta subject. 'Speaking of kidding' as a non-sequitur transition is both clumsy and revealing. Character-specific: Michael's earnestness always overrides his situational awareness.

jokes.text: Let me ask you something. How would you feel if I came down on you hard? — You're saying I'm not reading this right. — No, I'm saying I need to jump on you to break my fall.
jokes.setup: Michael has just asked how serious Gob is about Marta, implying he might want to pursue her
jokes.punchline: Gob's question ('how would you feel if I came down on you hard?') is heard by Michael — and the audience — as a response to the Marta question (a threat), but it's literally about jumping off the prison wall onto Michael
jokes.explanation: A brilliant double-meaning construction. 'How would you feel if I came down on you hard?' works perfectly as both (a) a brotherly threat about Marta and (b) a literal physical request to jump from a prison wall. The beat where Michael misreads it ('you're saying I'm not reading this right') and then the clarification is structured with textbook precision. Approaches the 8.0 anchor in execution.

jokes.text: The guards soon became concerned about the paperwork that would be required of them if Gob actually did jump.
jokes.setup: Gob is on top of the prison wall threatening to jump onto Michael; guards are watching
jokes.punchline: The guards' motivation for intervention is not concern for human safety but the administrative burden of the resulting paperwork
jokes.explanation: The narrator's bureaucratic explanation is dark and perfectly deadpan. Reducing a potential human death to a paperwork problem is the kind of institutional indifference that reads as both satirical and very funny. Another example of the narrator actively undercutting the apparent meaning of a scene.

jokes.text: I'm a complete failure. — Where'd you get that kind of talk? — From you. You always say that about me.
jokes.setup: Gob has failed to escape prison and is wallowing in defeat; George Sr. tries to be encouraging
jokes.punchline: Gob reveals that 'you're a complete failure' is specifically what George Sr. has told him throughout his life — the encouraging father is the one who supplied the self-critical language
jokes.explanation: A genuinely dark joke with emotional truth. The setup appears to be George Sr. being supportive, which makes the reveal — that the self-destructive phrase came from him — particularly cutting. The three-line structure is airtight: Gob's self-flagellation, George's seeming concern, then the reversal. Strong character integration on both sides.

jokes.text: Well, maybe you're not entirely to blame. I haven't always been the best kind of father either.
jokes.setup: George Sr. has just been told he always calls Gob a complete failure
jokes.punchline: George Sr.'s response is 'maybe you're not entirely to blame' and 'I haven't always been the best kind of father' — a massive understatement that treats habitual emotional abuse as a minor parenting imperfection
jokes.explanation: The understatement is the joke — 'not the best kind of father' as a description of telling your child repeatedly he's a complete failure. George Sr.'s partial self-awareness is both funny and sad. Works better as setup for the ball-throwing payoff that follows.

jokes.text: You've done a pretty good job of being a father to everybody in here. What have they got that I don't? You've never even thrown a ball around with me.
jokes.setup: George Sr. tries to excuse himself as a father; Gob points out that George coaches other inmates
jokes.punchline: Gob's wounded observation that his father has been a better father figure to prison inmates than to his own son — specifically invoking the catch moment from earlier
jokes.explanation: A callback that lands with emotional weight. The joke is dark — Gob genuinely means this — but the framing (prison inmates as preferred children over his actual son) is absurdly dark comedy. The ball-throwing detail was planted early and the payoff here is satisfying.

jokes.text: Great. Now you're an athlete.
jokes.setup: Gob has just expressed genuine hurt about never playing catch with his father
jokes.punchline: George Sr.'s sarcastic deflection — treating Gob's emotional vulnerability as an athletic affectation — instead of addressing the hurt
jokes.explanation: George Sr.'s instinct when confronted with emotion is to deflect with mockery. 'Great. Now you're an athlete.' is cutting precisely because it dismisses the vulnerability by recasting it as pretension. Strong character integration — this is exactly how George responds to his children's emotional needs.

jokes.text: I wanna be with you, Lindsay. — What? — And I know you wanna be with me too. — No, I don't. — But all the things you said about commitment and believing in things — that's the way I feel! — Johnny, I'm sorry. I didn't mean to lead you on. I think you're gross.
jokes.setup: Johnny Bark has been bonding with Lindsay in the tree over shared idealism; Lindsay has been confiding about her lost passion and her marriage
jokes.punchline: Lindsay's flat, devastating rejection: 'I think you're gross.' — No softening, no apology beyond the perfunctory, just immediate honesty about her physical disgust
jokes.explanation: The joke is in Lindsay's complete absence of tact. She apologizes for leading him on and then immediately confirms the worst: 'I think you're gross.' The cringe comes from the contrast between the intimate moment they just shared and the brutality of the rejection. Peak Lindsay character comedy — she means to be kind but the honesty is devastating.

jokes.text: Uh-oh. / My tree. [Johnny Bark sees that the tree is being destroyed while he was away from it]
jokes.setup: Johnny Bark came down from the tree to pursue Lindsay. The tree was only protected while he was in it.
jokes.punchline: The moment Bark left the tree to profess his love — having been lured down by Lindsay's apparent passion for the cause — the bulldozers moved in. His romantic pursuit destroyed his environmental mission.
jokes.explanation: A well-structured ironic payoff — Lindsay's oblivious romantic bonding in the tree indirectly caused the tree's destruction by drawing Bark away from it. The timing is perfect. 'My tree' as a two-word lament captures Bark's loss completely. The show doesn't over-explain the joke.

jokes.text: That's why you never get out of the tree. [after Johnny Bark abandons his tree protest to pursue Lindsay, and the bulldozers immediately knock the tree down]
jokes.setup: Johnny Bark has left his tree to follow Lindsay; the tree is immediately demolished without him in it
jokes.punchline: Michael's calm lesson about protest strategy — implying the fundamental error of any tree-sitter is ever leaving the tree — delivered while watching it fall
jokes.explanation: The timing and structure are perfect. The visual of the tree immediately falling the moment Johnny Bark leaves is the punchline; Michael's line is the cherry on top. 'That's why you never get out of the tree' works as both literal protest advice and a commentary on Lindsay's entire role in the situation. Economical and well-earned.

jokes.text: And for the first time, Gob had the game of catch with his father he'd been waiting for his whole life. [George Sr. and Gob throw a ball in prison] — White power! — I'm... white.
characters: White Power Bill / Dirty Ears Bill
jokes.setup: Gob has achieved his lifelong wish: playing catch with his father. The narrator frames it as an emotional milestone.
jokes.punchline: White Power Bill (now Dirty Ears Bill) shouts 'White power!' in the background; Gob responds with the deflated 'I'm... white.' — the heartwarming father-son moment is immediately undercut by the presence of the prison's white supremacist
jokes.explanation: A multilayered callback that perfectly resolves two simultaneous storylines (the catch and Dirty Ears Bill) while being darkly funny. The narrator's sincere 'he'd been waiting for his whole life' sets up genuine emotion, then 'White power!' shatters it. Gob's response — 'I'm... white' — is the perfect confused non-answer from a man still trying to fit in with his prison community. The juxtaposition of sentiment and absurdity is quintessential Arrested Development.

jokes.text: You guys don't have a machine, huh? [phone machine interrupts Michael's confession to Marta]
jokes.setup: Michael is about to confess his feelings to Marta; the moment is building toward something genuine
jokes.punchline: A phone call interrupts — and the caller comments on the lack of an answering machine — deflating the romantic moment entirely with mundane reality
jokes.explanation: A functional interruption gag. The comedy is in the timing — maximum romantic tension punctured by maximum mundanity. 'You guys don't have a machine, huh?' is a good line but the joke type (interruption at crucial moment) is well-worn.

jokes.text: Gob has been stabbed in the back. [narrator, then revealed literally: Gob is in the hospital with a stab wound]
jokes.setup: Michael learns Gob is in the hospital; the narrator announces 'Gob has been stabbed in the back'
jokes.punchline: The phrase works on two levels simultaneously — Gob has metaphorically been betrayed (by Michael not bringing the stairs), AND he has literally been stabbed in the back in prison
jokes.explanation: Arrested Development at its structural finest. The narrator's line operates simultaneously as idiom (betrayal) and literal fact (physical stabbing), and both readings are valid within the plot. The wordplay is not a cheap pun — it's built into the episode's narrative architecture. The rewatch bonus is high: on first viewing you might hear the idiom; on rewatch you understand it was always literal.

jokes.text: ? Ta-dah? [Gob, in a hospital bed, weakly attempting a magician's flourish]
jokes.setup: Gob is in the hospital after being stabbed; Michael arrives
jokes.punchline: Gob's instinct — even flat on his back with a stab wound — is to perform. The question-mark 'ta-dah?' signals he's not sure if this counts as a success
jokes.explanation: The question mark on 'Ta-dah?' does enormous work. This is pure character comedy: Gob survived something (he's not sure what) and his first instinct is to claim it as a performance. The uncertainty — did he escape? was this the trick? — is expressed in one syllable. Exceptional character integration. Only Gob Bluth ends up in the hospital and tries to take a bow.

jokes.text: So, listen, Buster, have you given any thought to my offer? — I'm sorry. What? — Well, my phone hasn't made a peep.
jokes.setup: Lucille Austero is following up on the romantic note she sent Buster at the Desi awards
jokes.punchline: Buster has no idea what offer she means — he couldn't read the note (no glasses), didn't understand it, or has forgotten it entirely — while she's been waiting by the phone
jokes.explanation: The cringe is in the asymmetry — she's been waiting for his call; he doesn't know an offer exists. The comedy is gentle here, mostly setup for the Buster/Lucille Austero relationship to develop.

jokes.text: Has your hair... always been that pointy, Miss Austero? — It's Lucille. — Right. Right. That's my mom's name.
jokes.setup: Buster, finally looking at Lucille Austero up close, seems to recognize something about her
jokes.punchline: Buster notices her hair is 'pointy' — confirming she is the 'brownish area with points' — and then the reveal that her name is Lucille lands as an ironic punchline: he's pursuing the exact replica of his mother
jokes.explanation: A perfect structural callback. 'Has your hair always been that pointy?' confirms to the audience that this IS the brownish area with points — Buster has found his blur. Then 'It's Lucille' / 'That's my mom's name' delivers the ironic punchline: Buster's great escape from his mother has led him directly to a woman named Lucille. The audience understands this before Buster does, making the 'Yes!' he clearly feels even funnier. One of the episode's best constructed moments.

jokes.text: He's fine. I'll be in the hospital bar. — Uh, you know there isn't a hospital bar, Mother. — Well, this is why people hate hospitals.
jokes.setup: Chaos has broken out in the hospital hallway; Lucille announces she'll be at the hospital bar
jokes.punchline: Michael informs her there isn't one; Lucille's response — 'this is why people hate hospitals' — treats the lack of a bar as the primary flaw of medical institutions
jokes.explanation: A brilliant character joke. Lucille's certainty that hospitals have bars, and her pivot to blaming the hospital for not having one, is perfectly in character. 'This is why people hate hospitals' as a sweeping sociological claim about bar policy is absurd and completely true to Lucille. Only she would have this specific complaint. High quotability.

jokes.text: You were right, Michael. Life is too short. Mine almost ended today. So I think I'm gonna work things out with Marta.
jokes.setup: Gob, having been stabbed in prison due to his own reckless stunt, has apparently reflected on mortality
jokes.punchline: Gob's near-death insight is that he should stay with the girlfriend he's been trying to escape — not as growth, but as the same self-centered logic applied to a new conclusion
jokes.explanation: The joke is that Gob has 'learned' the exact opposite lesson Michael wanted him to learn, at the exact moment Michael has fallen for Marta. The dramatic irony is high (we know Michael now wants Marta). Rewatch bonus because this resolves Michael's entire evening of hope.

jokes.text: Hmm. You know what's funny? — Mmm. — I was gonna walk out of that prison last night and break up with her. Good thing you didn't bring the stairs, huh?
jokes.setup: Michael is devastated that Gob is staying with Marta; Gob is blissfully unaware of Michael's feelings
jokes.punchline: 'Good thing you didn't bring the stairs, huh?' — Gob credits Michael's accidental failure to bring the stair car as the reason he (Gob) survived to recommit to Marta, which is devastating for Michael
jokes.explanation: An exceptional irony construction. The stair car — a running joke all episode — is now the accidental mechanism that prevented Gob from escaping and breaking up with Marta, which means Michael's neglect inadvertently kept the woman he loves with his brother. 'Good thing you didn't bring the stairs, huh?' is Gob being cheerful about something that is quietly destroying Michael. Rewatch bonus is high — this line recontextualizes the stair car across the entire episode.

jokes.text: Mmm. Very funny. Yeah. It's hysterical. I'm lucky.
jokes.setup: Gob has just said 'good thing you didn't bring the stairs, huh?' — completely oblivious to Michael's feelings for Marta
jokes.punchline: Michael's flat, devastated response — 'very funny, hysterical, I'm lucky' — all delivered without any of those qualities being true
jokes.explanation: Michael's suppressed devastation delivered as dry agreement is a good character beat. The line does double duty — it's both funny to the audience who knows the truth and plausible to Gob who doesn't. Solid but not exceptional.

jokes.text: Well, you know, you gotta put family first. That's the stupid thing that I believe.
jokes.setup: Marta thanks Michael for helping out the previous night; Michael explains why he did it
jokes.punchline: 'That's the stupid thing that I believe' — Michael immediately and accurately undercuts his own stated values, acknowledging that his core principle has just caused him enormous pain
jokes.explanation: Michael's self-awareness here is both funny and genuinely touching. 'That's the stupid thing that I believe' is a perfect encapsulation of his character — he knows putting family first is costing him personally, and he does it anyway. The self-deprecation is earned because the episode has just demonstrated exactly how 'stupid' it is. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: You two keep talking out there, I'm gonna start getting jealous.
jokes.setup: Michael and Marta are having a genuine moment of connection just outside Gob's hospital room
jokes.punchline: Gob's cheerful jealous comment — completely unable to perceive that his joke is literally true, that something real IS developing between Michael and Marta
jokes.explanation: The dramatic irony is the joke — Gob accidentally says the true thing while treating it as a throwaway. Works on rewatch when you know where the Michael/Marta arc goes.

jokes.text: I am so happy. I'm gonna call the kids.
jokes.setup: Marta is overjoyed that Gob has recommitted to their relationship — 'we're family now'
jokes.punchline: Marta's joy is the punchline from Michael's perspective — we cut to/from Michael's devastated face as she celebrates the thing that has just crushed him
jokes.explanation: The comedy is entirely structural — Marta's sincerity is the tragic punchline for Michael. Not a strong joke on its own but functions as an important beat in the episode's tonal payoff.

jokes.text: I've made a huge mistake. [Michael, alone, after realizing Gob is keeping Marta]
jokes.setup: Michael has done the right thing all episode — for family — and it has resulted in him losing the woman he's fallen for to his brother
jokes.punchline: Michael uses Gob's exact phrase — 'I've made a huge mistake' — completing the callback and making it now apply to Michael instead of Gob
jokes.explanation: The episode's best joke and one of Arrested Development's finest structural comedy moments. The callback is earned on multiple levels: (1) the phrase was established as Gob's in the same episode; (2) it now applies to Michael with perfect irony — Michael's mistake was not leaving Gob to jump off the wall, i.e., doing the right thing is the huge mistake; (3) it's the emotional capper of the entire episode; (4) it plants the phrase that becomes one of the show's most iconic recurring lines. The economy is absolute — four words close the episode's thematic arc. This is what 9.5+ craft looks like: the joke works on first watch, works better on rewatch, serves character, serves plot, and becomes a cultural reference.

jokes.text: Looks like the shiv would have done some real damage if it hadn't been for this. It was in your lower intestine. So close. [the key Gob swallowed is revealed to have deflected the shiv]
jokes.setup: 'On the next Arrested Development' — Gob learns how he survived being stabbed
jokes.punchline: The key Gob swallowed — which created his entire prison predicament — is the exact object that saved his life when the shiv hit it in his intestine. 'So close.' as the kicker.
jokes.explanation: A spectacular structural payoff that operates as both a 'next episode' tease and a complete joke in itself. The key — swallowed as part of an idiotic magic stunt — saved Gob's life by being in exactly the wrong place at exactly the right time. 'So close' is delivered as an afterthought but lands as a dark kicker: the doctor is acknowledging the near-miss of the shiv. The joke rewards every viewer who remembered the key-swallowing from Act One. This is what exceptional structural comedy looks like.

jokes.text: Looks like the shiv would have done some real damage if it hadn't been for this. [holds up the swallowed key] / It was in your lower intestine. / So close.
jokes.setup: In the 'on the next' tag, a doctor shows Gob what saved his life.
jokes.punchline: The key Gob swallowed — and spent the episode desperately trying to pass — stopped the prison shiv. The key that was causing him so much digestive distress accidentally saved his life. 'So close' (to where it needed to exit) is the final dark laugh.
jokes.explanation: An exceptional 'on the next' tag — the key swallowing subplot resolves in the most satisfying and absurd way possible: the thing causing Gob distress all episode was also saving his life. The structure is nearly perfect — 'so close' (to passing the key) doubles as 'so close' (to being killed). This is Arrested Development's signature layered payoff, where a running physical comedy bit becomes the episode's emotional and structural resolution.

jokes.text: Buster. — Let's go for it. [Buster makes a bold romantic move toward Lucille Austero]
jokes.setup: 'On the next Arrested Development' — Buster makes his move on Lucille Austero
jokes.punchline: 'Let's go for it' — Buster's sudden uncharacteristic confidence, deployed toward a woman who is essentially his mother's clone and named Lucille
jokes.explanation: The kicker on the Buster arc. 'Let's go for it' from the most timid, mother-dependent character in the show is funny because of the contrast. Rewatch bonus: this brief tease pays off in the next episode. Works as a comedic cliffhanger.

tmdb_overview: Gob decides to lock himself in his father's prison for 24 hours to get back in with the alliance, and to spend quality time with his father. Michael takes Gob's girlfriend to an awards show, and Buster inadvertently flirts with his mother's rival.
insight: 51 jokes in 21 minutes—Arrested Development hits its stride with relentless character-driven absurdism.
